content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 22, 2018

Thomas Watson
City Attorney
City Attorney's Office
333 Civic Center Plaza
Tracy, CA 95376

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-150

Dear Mr. Watson:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Tracy Planning
Commissioner Chad Wood regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act"). Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions
of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict
of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and
accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should
contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit Commissioner Wood from participating in a decision related to a draft
ordinance for the City of Tracy that would establish locational and development application
requirements for medical cannabis delivery businesses where his law practice provided legal
services to a former client in connection with a cannabis distribution business located in another
city?

CONCLUSION

No. As explained below, the decision relating to the draft ordinance will not have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Commissioner's interest in his law practice
as a business entity or his former client as a source of income.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-150
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are seeking advice on behalf of Commissioner Wood concerning the Planning
Commission's upcoming consideration of an amendment to the City's zoning ordinance regarding
locational and development application requirements for medical cannabis non-storefront (delivery
only) retailers in the City.

On December 5, 2017, the City Council directed staff to prepare ordinances and regulations
to allow a maximum of two (2) medical cannabis non-storefront (delivery only) retailers to operate
in the City and a ballot measure to impose a tax on all cannabis commercial activity in the City. Its
specific direction was that these retailers be located in industrial areas of the City and be subject to
City regulations as well as local land use and/or regulatory permit(s).

These proposed City requirements for cannabis businesses will be in addition to the
requirements established under the state Medicinal and Adult Use Cannabis Regulation and Safety
Act ("MAUCRSA"). Under MAUCRSA, a cannabis business seeking a state license to operate
must show compliance with any requirements imposed by the local jurisdiction in which their
business is located as a condition of obtaining licensure.

The draft ordinance proposes locational requirements that provide distance buffers between
these cannabis retailers and certain uses considered to be sensitive, beyond those identified in state
law, such as public parks and residential zones. The draft ordinance also contains a requirement that
a cannabis retailer obtain a conditional use permit for their specific site prior to commencing
operations. These proposed requirements require an amendment to the City's zoning ordinance.

Commissioner Wood was appointed to the Commission on March 20, 2018. He is a licensed
attorney and one of two shareholders of a private law firm located in Tracy. In August 2017,
Commissioner Wood provided a legal consult to a cannabis business seeking legal advice regarding
establishing a cannabis distribution business. This business contacted Commissioner Wood in
February 2018 to obtain assistance with a real property transaction to secure a location for their
business in the City of Modesto. Commissioner Wood was compensated in excess of $500.00 for
the consultation and assistance with the property transaction. Commissioner Wood completed his
legal services for this business on March 30, 2018 and has not performed any additional legal
services since that date, nor has he been retained under contract by this business.

Commissioner Wood has no other clients engaged in the cannabis industry. You have no
information to suggest that the City's adoption of the draft ordinance allowing two cannabis
delivery businesses to operate in Tracy will positively or negatively financially effect
Commissioners Wood's law practice or his former clients. Commissioner Wood has stated that he
would likely decline any future cannabis related clients interested in doing business in Tracy.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from making, participating
in making, or using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision that would have
a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests.

File No. A-18-150
Page No. 3

distinguishable from the decision's effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An
official's financial interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act
are set forth in Section 87103 and include all of the following:

• Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or
more (subdivision (a)), or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee,
or holds any position of management (subdivision (d)).

• Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more.
(Subdivision (b).)

• Any source of income aggregating $500 or more in value to the official in the 12 months prior
to when the decision at issue is made. (Subdivision (c).)

• Any donor of a gift or gifts aggregating $470 or more to the official in the 12 months prior to
when the decision at issue is made. (Subdivision (e); Regulation 18940.2.)

• An interest in the official's personal finances or those of immediate family members.

For the decision at issue, Commissioner Wood has an interest in his law practice as a
business entity and source of income, an interest in any clients of the business, including the above-
mentioned cannabis business, from whom he has received income aggregating $500 or more in the
previous 12 months as sources of income, and an interest in his personal finances.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(b) provides that the effect of a decision on an official's interest that is not
a named party in, or the subject of, that decision, as here, is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical."

Regulation 18702.1 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to an interest in a business
entity not explicitly involved in a decision, including a source of income.² Regulation 18702.1(b)
provides that a decision's effect on an official's business interest is material "if a prudent person
with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial
effect would contribute to a change in the ... value of a privately-held business entity."

Commissioner Wood's Law Practice

The decision at issue concerns a proposed amendment to the City's zoning ordinance
regarding locational and development application requirements for medical cannabis non-storefront
(delivery only) retailers in the City. Although Commissioner Wood provided legal services to a
cannabis business regarding establishing a cannabis distribution business and securing property for
the business in Modesto, he has no other clients engaged in the cannabis industry and, in any event,
will decline future cannabis related clients interested in doing business in Tracy. Moreover, the

² See Regulation 18702.3(a)(4).

File No. A-18-150
Page No. 4

facts state you have no information suggesting adoption of the draft ordinance will have any
financial effect on Commissioner Wood's law practice.

Cannabis Business Client

As mentioned, the proposed amendment to the City's zoning ordinance concerns various
requirements related to medical cannabis delivery only retailers in the City of Tracy. However, the
facts state that Commissioner Wood's client obtained legal services related to a cannabis
distribution business for whom he then helped secure real property in a different city, Modesto, for
their business. In addition, you stated there is no information to suggest adoption of the draft
ordinance will have any financial effect on this former client of Commissioner Wood.

Therefore, the decision relating to the draft ordinance will not have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on the Commissioner's interest in his law practice as a business entity or
his former client as a source of income.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Acting General Counsel

By: Jack Woodside
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit Commissioner Wood from participating in a decision related to a draft 
ordinance for the City of Tracy that would establish locational and development application 
requirements for medical cannabis delivery businesses where his law practice provided legal 
services to a former client in connection with a cannabis distribution business located in another 
city?
sections.conclusion:
No. As explained below, the decision relating to the draft ordinance will not have a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Commissioner's interest in his law practice 
as a business entity or his former client as a source of income.
